1. Purpose
This document provides the guideline for  creation of bare flex independent QCP and CPP.  
2. Scope
This document applies for new project or process changes which are not applicable for items/ 
contents in general QCP and CPP in 080-01409 and 080-03022
There should be maximum one independent QCP/CPP per project, shared across all vendors 
building that project.
3. Responsibilities
DFM : Review design and extract uniqueness of new product or changes.  Review with PD for 
design proposal and finalize design. 
Application SQE:  Product realization ,define Process flow and  reflecting process controls and 
requirements into independent QCP and CPP .
Site SQE : Follow up QCP&CPP execution , issue feedback 
GSM: Follow up with supplier to be in place resource needed in terms of new CAPEX , human 
resource , capacity planning .
4. References
080-01409	
CPP Apple bare FPC critical process parameter 
080- 03022       QCP Apple bare FPC Quality Control Plan 
080-02999         CQRA Apple   continuous Quality Reliability Assurance   
5. Definitions
CPP : Apple bare FPC critical process parameter 
QCP : Apple bare FPC Quality Control Plan 
DFMEA  : Design Failure mode and Effects Analysis
6. Policy Statement
Independent QCP& CPP states compulsory requirements for quality and process 
capability related .If suppliers are not compliance to this requirement, supplier has to 
submit corrective action to Apple team .

If supplier have noncompliance to independent QCP& CPP ,refere to document 
<Guideline for Risk assessment and action for violation at requirement for further 
action>.
7. Procedure
7.1. Opportunities of creation for independent QCP and 
CPP  
•    When start new project. 
•   Process change  
7.2. Design /new requirement review 
•
DFM should take lead to review design if it is manufacturable an feasible for product 
realization , raise the proposal to optimize design
• Application SQE should take lead to review new requirement for product .
7.3. DFMEA
• DFM/application SQE should take lead to consolidate and review DFMEA to de-risk 
all the new requirements and processes
7.4. Review with suppliers
• Application SQE should review with supplier to collect data and feedback
• Application SQE should review and drive flex vendor for DFMEA to de-risk new 
requirement /new design
7.5. Create independent QCP & CPP
• Application SQE should extract information from DFMEA and design review to 
define process control and process flow.
• Application SQE should create QCP and CPP , this document focus on unique items 
which are not stated in general QCP and CPP.
• Applicate SQE can integrate QCP and CPP , see appendix for template example , 
the document should include IQC and In process control /OQC and CQRA and 
CPP .if there is item unique from general QCP and CPP,  application SQE should 
highlight in this document. 
7.6. DFM team/ fab team final review 
•  Application SQE should have final review with DFM/fab for more inputs to finalize 
document .     
7.7. Communicate with GSM team
• Review with GSM team to understand requirement in term of resource ,new 
CAPEX needed , so that GSM team can work for readiness in suppliers’ side .

• Application SQE should release document through agile and get document no. 
7.9. Follow up for execution  
• Application SQE should  organize a review meeting with QCP/CPP by suppliers
• Site SQE should be invited .
• site SQE should follow each supplier sites for execution of independent QCP/CPP
• Suppliers should convert requirements into internal document .
• Site SQE should drive for FACA on noncompliance item or refer to guideline for 
Risk assessment and action for violation at requirement for further action

2.4 Flex CQRA-bare flex
	
( Refer to the latest SOP for  Apple bare flex CQRA - defined in ok2ship )  
         1. Reflow test :   ok to skip pre-conditioning as Nikkan material is unable to meet that . 
         2. thermal stress/hot oil : acceptable to survive  at 260 c  
         3.FIB /IM : monitoring, acceptable for nano-crac 
         4. Test coupon design ( CQRA) should be submitted for apple approval before build started ( refer 
to D4X design rule )  
         5. waive for b-Hast for Dupont HXC-1225 ( failed for b-Hast in D4X )  
2.5 Critical Process Parameters - Bare Flex (Follow  Apple bare flex CPP , otherwise 
highlight as below )
  
 1.  Via Drilling  process 
• Machine type : UV laser ( compulsory )
• Drilling scraps removal ( 2mm guide hole ) : preferred by vacuum removal by machine instead of human  
 2. Copper plating 
• Plating line ( outlayer) :  Via filling by VCP (compulsory ) 
• Plating line ( inner layer ) : R2R plating , preferred : via filling if have 
 3. Via AVI and AOM
• AVI ( outlayer/inner layer ) :  100%
• AOM ( outlayer/inner layer ) :10%
• Clean room level : 10K less preferred. 
 4. button pad imaging 
• Exposure ( outlayer) :  Preferred LDI , or proper qualification if by CCD 
 5. Patterning
• Exposure  ( outlayer) :  LDI
• Exposure  ( inner layer ) : R2R CCD 
 6. Etching 
• Etching line ( outlayer) :  preferred 2 fluid etching + vacuum
• Etching line ( inner layer ) : preferred 2 fluid etching + vacuum
 7. De-cap
• use pico-laser to have v-cut on transition area .
• De-capping by machine .
 8. Solder mask
• Exposure :  LDI,  Manual registration/CCD are not allowed. 
• Curing : box oven is preferred , or proper qualification is conducted as benchmark of box oven for 
parameters setting. 
 9. Lamination 
• Bonding sheet must be seasoning under dryer oven for 12 hours min back to room temperature.
(follow data sheet)
• L2/L3 must be pre-treated with micro-etching and bake160c @2hours with N2 before lamination.
•  All the L1 and L4 before  IL layup must be baked for 2 hours at 160c/N2 to remove moisture .
• Full lamination condition : 180c at 1.5hours + post baking after de-capping at 180C 1.5 hours.
• Final lamination ( SUS/PI) should be placed after Mic hole forming ???
 10. Micro-etching before layup

Page    of 
4

1.
Purpose
The purpose of this document is to define the Quality control requirements. Manufacturer needs 
to follow the IQC/IPQC and product specification requirements for specified Apple designs. The 
QCP is the direction for flex Manufacturer to develop quality control.
During NPI/Engineering Builds, if there is any deviation on process from this QCP, Manufacturer  
needs to feedback to authorized Apple representative and raise the deviation report in OK2Ship 
report. Authorized Apple representative will review and provide the deposition.   
After P2 build, no change is allowable by manufacturer without qualification plans agreement, 
PCN submission and Authorized Apple representative Approval.
2. Scope
This document applies as the general guidelines of Quality Control of bare flexible printed circuit 
boards for use within Apple products. The guidelines clarifies process quality requirements
This document applies standards to Bare FPC within Apple products. This document should be 
applied as the standard for flex Manufacturer  in quality control at IQC/IPQC/OQC and other 
relevant quality activities throughout all build stage, including prototype and mass production.
3. Responsibilities
This document establishes the quality control request for Flexible Printed Circuit Fabrication. 
FPC Manufacturer  Quality Manager must implement the control requirements.
This document is to be considered a guideline for the quality control of bare FPC fabrication, it is 
the responsibility of FPC manufacturer  to maintain quality to meet Apple requirement. The 
quality check item not mentioned in this document should be refer to individual project QCP 
issued by respective SQE.
And Individual FPC Manufacturers must have their individual control plan which include more 
control items than the list provided below since below is the only critical control items.
Deviations to this document must be approved by Apple Flex SQE prior to implementation.
On the NPI/Engineering Build, If there is any deviation on process control in the build, 
Manufacturer  to feedback to SQE and raise the deviation report in OK2Ship report. SQE will 
review and provide the deposition. 
Apple will maintain the authority for final disposition of all defective FPC fabricated by the FPC 
manufacturer.

Dwg Number:  080-03022
Rev: P
Page    of 
7
53
6. Quality tools requirements
6.1.SPC Control Charts (X̅-R, X-MR, …)
A. All process/product control parameters (Chemical Concentration…) must follow all SPC rules 
(as stated below) and charts (I-MR, X̅-R, X̅-S and P-Chart for defects). 
• Rule 1: A single point beyond LCL/UCL.
• Rule 2: 9 consecutive points on the same side of the centerline.
• Rule 3: 6 consecutive points steadily increasing or decreasing.
• Rule 4: 14 or more consecutive points are alternating up and down.
B. Review frequency for UCL/LCL/CpK/Cp shall be once per month at the minimum and 3 
sigma rule with CpK > 1.33 shall be applied. If CpK is < 1.33, then 100% product sorting is 
needed.
C. P-Chart limits shall be calculated based on the 1st 30 lots data after EVT & reviewed every 
month thereafter. Initial limit for subsequent year project (NPI) shall be per previous year’s 
MP limits from the same project family.  
6.2.Laney P-chart control
A. For prototype stage, set the UCL with using the similar design and construction product 
B. From EVT stage onwards, renew the UCL base on Proto build result
C. For Mass Production stage, used the prototype stage product to set the initial UCL, follow by 
1000 sheet data and 1 month data. 
D. The P-Chart required to be reviewed minimum monthly to adjust the UCL

080-03022-P Apple Bare FPC Quality Control Plan

__data__.text:
THE INFORMATION CONTAINED HEREIN IS THE PROPERTY OF APPLE INC. THE POSSESSOR AGREES TO THE FOLLOWING:
(i) TO MAINTAIN THIS DOCUMENT IN CONFIDENCE (ii) NOT TO REPRODUCE OR COPY IT  (iii) NOT TO REVEAL OR PUBLISH IT IN WHOLE OR IN PART
 Apple Inc
Size: Letter
Scale: NONE
Title:  Apple Bare FPC Quality Control Plan
Dwg Number:  080-03022
Rev: P
Page    of 
8
53
6.3.Common Specifications and Requirements
A. All lines/Equipments/processes must be qualified following 080-02647 LQ guidelines prior to 
apply to production.
B. In-process rework and repair are forbidden without Apple SQE’s written approval. Below 
manual operations are acceptable:
• Manual decap/manual rework after automatic decap: at the conditions 1) SOP defines 
clearly steps and tools, and 2) VI confirms quality after decap (e.g. no residue, no damage, 
etc.) 
• Manual cleaning in process just before pre-treatment, and at pattern AOI: cleaning 
roller, cotton swab with or without IPA, or ethanol
• Manual cleaning at FG level: refer to SOP in 080-03269 cosmetic criteria.
C. FPC Manufacturer is not allowed to use any Apple config build for their internal DoE run and 
ship to Apple. FPC manufacturer must use different internal PN for internal DoE or test run.
D. FPC Manufacturers should follow Apple SQE requirement to submit quality requirements
• Build readiness review
• OK2ship Report 
• Post mortem review 
• Flow chart 
• PMP 
• FMEA 
• Others as needed
E. Tape used to cover openings in the panel after cut out IPQC coupons, etc. shall not 
contaminate conductive pads nearby.
6.4.Shop floor control, traceability, data retention
A. All necessary information which has potential quality risk should be tracked by flex vendors 
individually.
B. Traceability for Quality data and process data defined QCP and CPP need to be tracked but 
not limited to them only.
C. Factory data/information accommodated to individual vendors’ process which are not 
defined in CPP/QCP should be tracked.
D. AOI, AOM, AVI, and E-test data and result should be linked each other and be linked to 
downstream SMT/Backend process.
E.
All manufacturing documents, records, via and pattern AOI and VRS photos and objects 
subjected to destructive/reliability test shall be retained for three years minimum (e.g. cross 
section coupons, impedance coupons, CQRA coupons, etc.). Refer to 080-01409 general 
CPP for requirement details about their link to traceability shop floor system.
F.
Following information at relevant stations should be tracked but not limited to below only
• Legend: 1) Lot #, 2) Sheet #, 3) Cavity #, 4) Date code
• Employee number: 1) Technician, 2) Engineers, 3) Operators
• Quality Data: 1) IPQC Measurement data, 2) Via hole AOI/AOM data, 3) AOI data, 4) E-test 
data, 5) OQC data, 6) CQRA data, 7) Yield data, 8) FA data/report, 9) Equipment/
instrument calibration data/record
• Production Record
i.
Machine/Line #
ii.
Tooling/Fixture #

6.5.Maverick Lot Program
A. Maverick lot is defined as an abnormal/non conformance lot from a population of lots 
manufactured.
B. Definition of Maverick includes but not limited to:
a.
Product parameters unable to meet CpK of 1.33 and/or OOS. 
b.
Process parameters unable to meet SPC requirement stipulated in section SPC Control 
Charts
c.
Defect mode and/or total scrap % trend out of control on P-Chart. 
d.
Product unable to meet quality requirements defined in any Apple document. 
e.
Product produced not in accordance to standard operating procedure.
f.
Product produced not in accordance to equipment/tool standard operations. 
g. Product failed CQRA requirement
C. FPC vendor shall:
A. Do FA, OCAP, and decide disposition of each maverick lot separately. It is not 
acceptable to check only one representative lot.
B. Inform Apple SQE about root cause analysis and action proposal on all maverick lots 
fabricated, for final disposition approval in writing.
C. **Best Practice: maverick system sends email notification automatically to Apple SQE 
triggered by control limits/CQRA failures. The time that Apple SQE received notification 
shall be the same as vendor DRI.
D. Any panels or worksheets in wet lines that encountered non conforming process conditions 
shall be scrapped immediately, including but not limited to: panel jam anywhere in the line 
(loading/unloading modules, wet modules, drying modules, etc.), rack jam, electricity cut off, 
control parameters OOS, panels stopped temporarily in wet lines due to tape connection 
issue, etc.
E.

   of 
11
53
6.6.Process Change Notice (PCN)
A. Vendor triggered changes to system, rules, design, material and process are strictly halted 
after P2 for Apple FPC. Apple DFM/SQE shall be notified of any vendor proposed changes in 
accordance with the format and basic principles outlined in JESD46 and changes shall not 
be implemented prior to receipt of written approval from an authorized Apple representative. 
B. Example of changes are, but not limited to:
• Material that is permanent part of the finished product (copper foil, bonding sheet, cover 
layer, SM etc..)
• Any chemistry that react to become part of the permanent finished product.
• Manufacturing site
• Method of manufacturing (Process flow, mechanism, equipment, target value setting, 
process parameter etc..)
• Quality System
• AOI/AVI/VRS Setting
• Method/Contents at IQC/IPQC/OQC/CQRA
• Electrical (Open/Short), Leak 2 (Double Electrical Test), Threshold
• Scaling Management
• Panelization (panel size, no of array, orientation)
6.7.Failure Analysis (FA)
A. FPC vendor shall do physical failure analysis on failing units, including but not limited to:
a.
Spark 1&2 failures. FA should use thermal emission first, and then CT scan, X-ray, etc. 
Refer to electrical test section for details.
• NPI: 100% of failures
• MP: sampling following AQL G3 per APN per day.
b.
All CQRA testing failures including bHast failures. Refer to CQRA section for details.
B. FPC vendor shall include heat map to visualize trends by layer within panel or array (e.g. 
failure rate, FAI dimension, etc.) when publishing 8D FA reports, including but not limited to 
all inline failures, reliability failures, EFFA, yield improvement activities.
C. FPC vendor shall have non-polar liquid (diiodomethanue, or equivalent) available on site in 
order to check Water Contact Angle whenever necessary.

10.6.1.SUS/PI Stiffener peel strength / bond strength
10.6.1.1.Method: FPC vendor shall measure peel strength between the FPC and the 
stiffener with a 90º peel, at a constant speed of 57 mm/minute, and record 
average of data taken at the beginning, middle and end of the peel.
10.6.1.2.The peel strength between the FPC and the stiffener shall be a minimum of 
1.4 kg per 25mm width when bonded with thermoset adhesive. 
10.6.1.3.The peel strength between the FPC and the stiffener shall be a minimum of 
0.38 kg per 25mm width when bonded with pressure sensitive adhesive.
Stiffener Peel Strength test

Section 1. 
Document Description 
Scope 
 
This document applies to the design of flexible printed circuits for use within Apple products. The 
guidelines will be useful for members of Apple’s Product Design, PCB design and verification, and Design 
for Manufacturing (DFM) teams.  
Purpose 
 
The purpose of this document is to establish best practices for the design of flexible printed circuits for 
use in Apple, Inc. products. This document may also provide a useful reference for the state of flexible 
printed circuit vendor base capability. It is not intended to be and should not be used as an acceptability 
specification for finished goods.  
 
This document should be considered a supplement to document 080-0916 DFM Design Guideline. 
Questions about these design guidelines or their application should be directed to your DFM team 
member.  
Referenced Documents  
 
The latest revisions of documents noted below shall be applicable and will provide supplementary 
information about materials, tolerances, etc. for finished goods.

Definitions 
 
Definitions used in this specification are in accordance with IPC-T-50.   
ACF 
Anisotropic Conductive Film, used for fine pitch bonding 
LPI/SM/PSR 
Solder mask, a liquid coat that is photoimagable and prevents oxidation and solder shorting 
Table 1-2 Definitions 
Design Rules with “No DFM sign off required” 
 
Should be observed in all cases except when necessary. No DFM team member needs to sign off in order 
to deploy. This colloquially means “these are the rules that are safe to use everywhere.” 
Design rules with “DFM sign off required” 
 
The tightest possible design rules which are supported by a limited number of vendors and where the 
design specifics come into play. If any design must use these limit rules, immediate coordination with the 
DFM team and the flex GSM is required. 
Outline and format 
 
This document is organized into the main design ideas that comprise a flex, organized in alphabetical 
order. Items with deep interaction, such as Coverlay and LPI, are grouped together.

Section 3. 
Air Gaps 
Air Gaps for Increasing Flexibility  
As a last resort to increased flexibility, an air gap can be added to a 2+ layer flex, though beware that air 
gaps may induce issues with flex layer delamination. If a specific and tough bending requirement using 
an air gap is necessary, extensive testing should be done, so immediate coordination with DFM and GSM 
is required to implement.  
 
Figure 3-1 Air Gap added for additional two-layer flexibility

Section 4. 
Barcodes  
Barcodes and Traceability 
Apple requires barcodes on many of our components in order to help with traceability and organization at the 
factories. The vast majority of flex designs have barcodes, but not all. Make sure that different flex vendors make 
barcodes that the downstream vendors can read. Ideally, we want every component on the flex to be linked to 
every single flex’s serial number, so we can always get containment if a bad lot of components comes. We are not 
always able to do this, but this means that every component, like connectors, microphones, or chips, has a serial 
number to be connected to the flex serial number. The flex serial number should be scanned at module vendors or 
FATP to be linked to the module serial or system serial numbers, so make sure the barcode is in a location and side 
of the flex that the module vendor or FATP can scan. For all barcodes etched with a laser, there is a possible 
cosmetic concern that should be agreed upon before implementation between PD and DFM. Some locations may 
not be desired to have barcodes from ID perspective.  
We have a few main options for how to create them on the flex. See more info at document 081-2110C. 
Types of barcodes 
Type of barcode 
Size 
Notes 
Removable stickers 
(Paper/PET/Kapton) 
5x5mm or 
4x4mm 
(minimum is 
3.8x3.8mm) 
Often placed on PSA liners. The adhesive area can be specified and 
limited, like keeping half of the square without adhesive to aid 
barcode removal. These barcodes are removed during system 
assembly, along with the PSA liner. Usually white background with 
black ink, but it is possible to have black background with white ink. 
Our most common barcode. 
SUS barcode 
2x2mm 
IR laser directly burns barcode into the SUS. SUS barcodes are very 
common in use. Other sizes are available, like rectangles. Visible grain 
directions in the SUS lead to low quality barcodes, so the SUS should 
appear as a smooth surface to the eye to get a good barcode.  
Note: cannot use C7701 alloy because there is low contrast and high 
cycle time. Also, can’t laser on DT-4, as DT-4 SUS will be easily get 
rusted at the engraved pattern. 
Silkscreen background 
(to be laser etched) 
3.5x3.5mm 
(minimum is 
3x3mm) 
White silkscreen square is UV laser etched with a 2.5x2.5mm barcode, 
leaving a corresponding 0.5mm “quiet zone” for readability. No 
bending allowed on barcode. This has a +/- 0.5mm positional 
tolerance. Use as a last resort. The size can go down to 3.3mm x 
3.3mm in special cases.

Note: cannot use C7701 alloy because there is low contrast and high 
cycle time. Also, can’t laser on DT-4, as DT-4 SUS will be easily get 
rusted at the engraved pattern. 
Silkscreen background 
(to be laser etched) 
3.5x3.5mm 
(minimum is 
3x3mm) 
White silkscreen square is UV laser etched with a 2.5x2.5mm barcode, 
leaving a corresponding 0.5mm “quiet zone” for readability. No 
bending allowed on barcode. This has a +/- 0.5mm positional 
tolerance. Use as a last resort. The size can go down to 3.3mm x 
3.3mm in special cases. 
Inkjet background (to 
be laser etched) 
3x3mm 
White ink put onto flex with 0.5mm “quiet zone”. Bending is OK in this 
region. +/- 0.3mm positional tolerance, with a 0.45mm “quiet zone” 
where there is no laser etching allowed. No vias with button plating 
are allowed in the barcode region because flatness is key to the inkjet

Bend Area Keep-out Guide 
 
Figure 5-2 Bend Area Keep-out Guide for dynamic bends 
 
In order to determine the dynamic bend area keep-out zone, take the value determined for X from the chart above 
and add it to each side of the bend area.  
All functional features such as solder pads, plated holes, contact pads must stay within the green zone on the 
bending angle chart above. All unnecessary or redundant features including exposed ground and bonded stiffeners 
will be allowed in the yellow zone. The red zone is a 100% keep-out area. 
Absolutely no functional vias (PTH, NPTHs, blind vias, or buried vias) are to be in the bending area.

Assembly Ease 
A stiffener will allow for a more precise bend location for ease of assembly. Note: ensure bend region is of large 
enough size to allow for stiffeners to be placed closely. Contact your DFM DRI for more information.

Increasing Flexibility  
One option is to add a slit to the flex which reduces torsional torque and deflection.

As a last resort to increased flexibility, adding an air gap can work on multilayer flexes, though air gaps may induce 
issues with delamination. Please see Air Gap section for more information.  
 
Copper Selection 
Select HA or HA-V2 copper for Critical Bend angle/radius, not ED or RA copper. See Copper Foils and Copper 
Plating section for more information. 
Bend Test Requirements 
The flex circuits that require bending will require bend testing and this requirement should be stated on the MCO 
or CPP.  
A common bend test is 25 bend cycles from flat to bent to flat with no loss of signal integrity using the actual flex 
bend radius and bend angle required for that specific part in the MCO. Specific requirements vary based on flex 
design and final application. Please see Bare FPC CQRA document 080-02999 for more details.  
Bend test requirements should be on MCO if PD needs pre-bend. See MCO and Flex Design Best Practices section 
for more information.

Coverlay opening shapes 
Openings in coverlay material are typically formed by punch prior to lamination to the FPC.  As a result, registration 
between the coverlay openings and copper pattern is not precise and the size and spacing between adjacent 
openings have hard lower limits.  Additionally, adhesive squeeze out into the opening reduces precision. 
Therefore, coverlay may not be suitable for fine-pitch components. Finally, coverlay openings should be simple 
shapes, not odd or meandering shapes. See Coverlay section for more information. See figure below and note the 
complexity of the pink opening compared to the yellow opening.

Soldermask and Coverlay Web Design 
The minimum distance allowed between a series of copper pads such as a board to board (B2B) connector or a ZIF 
connector (for example) is determined by the method we use to create the solder dams that are required to 
eliminate solder bridging. The minimum distance between the contact pads when using copper defined pads and 
soldermask is 0.30mm, which include the actual, solder dam (0.10mm) and the spacing (0.05mm), which is 
required for registration of the soldermask. The distance between contact pads using soldermask defined pads can 
be as low as 0.15mm however there is no allowance for registration which can compromise the exposed pad size. 
See Figure 6-13 below. 
Coverlay requires a wider web and increased spacing because producing web smaller than 0.40mm is very difficult 
and coverlay registration is also a paramount concern. See image below

Coverlay to LPI Overlap 
 
LPI and coverlay must overlap when they come in contact with each other to make sure the vulnerable copper 
underneath is always protected. In addition, whether solder mask overlaps the coverlay or the coverlay overlaps 
the solder mask is a vendor preference.  
Section 7. 
De-cap 
When a flex needs to have different numbers of copper layers for different regions of the same flex, de-cap is 
required at the layer transition to cut away the excess layers. For example, a flex may have two layers of copper for 
half and be only one layer for the other half, with the layer transition being the area for de-cap. The cut away 
process is done with either a pre-punch of PI and bonding sheet (usually only for 3+ layer flexes) before lamination 
or with a laser after lamination. 
 
De-cap regions can generally be broken down into two main categories: layer transitions with a nearby bend and 
those without bends.

De-cap categorical guidelines 
Item Description  
Related page 
1 
All flexes where a layer transition/de-cap is necessary must have DFM sign off. 
 
2 
The coverlay and adhesive that go through the transition region need to be thick 
enough to account for the thickness of the PI and bonding adhesive. This prevents the 
coverlay from getting torn on the edge of the base PI on the step down. This also 
applies for Silver EMI shielding too. 
 
3 
Because the de-cap is covered by coverlay to prevent copper oxidation, the 
dimensions cannot be directly measured after coverlay application so all FAI/SPC 
measurements must be done in process. It must be measured in process or only as a 
reference value. 
  
4 
De-cap transition cannot be in the bend zone. Because the transition line will also have 
a change in stiffness, the bend might end up on the transition line anyway if they are 
too close. 
 
5 
Coverlay should either end before the transition line or after the de-cap line.  
 
6 
For ease of manufacturability, the de-cap/transition line should be a straight line. If 
this is not possible, it is best to keep the angle change to 45° or less between straight 
lines. 
 
7 
PI stiffeners can overlap de-cap transition zones 
 
8 
A “pseudo-decap” can be made by etching away the copper on one layer but keeping 
the PI base. This thins the flex and reduces the stiffness but does not require the 
complexity of normal de-cap transitions. These are sometimes used to help form tight 
bends.

See 080-2265 for more details on drill tolerances and dimensional rules. 
Drill sizes do not represent the actual finished hole size.  The finished hole size will vary with plating thickness and 
the actual drill diameter (or in the case of laser, the aperture size) used.  In general, suppliers should be expected 
to use the largest available drill diameter that will fit the existing pad diameter.  As such, the pad diameter should 
be viewed as the constraint. 
 
Laser vias and plated through holes require a certain plating aspect in order to be plated with consistent quality. 
The plating aspect ratio is ratio of flex thickness to drill size. This becomes more important when plated through 
holes are used on thicker flexes. Consult with your flex DFM engineer to determine the proper drill size for the flex.

Section 9. 
Encapsulants and Conformal Coatings 
Encapsulants and Conformal Coating areas  
Conformal coatings and encapsulations are used as a moisture barrier, for preventing water ingress, or for 
additional mechanical strength. Selection of conformal coating type may vary upon the application requirement. 
Conformal coating flow range and height coverage may vary with components profile, number of components and 
the surrounding condition. 
An area 0.90mm beyond the edges of the component and height 0.3mm (0402 or smaller) or 0.4mm (0603 or 
larger) are required to ensure complete component coverage. 
For components near bends, make sure to keep the conformal coating at least 0.5mm away from the bend to 
prevent cracking.

Section 10. Etched Line and Space for Traces 
Single-sided FPC  
The basic design rules of trace and space as well as via and pad will vary with the overall layer structure, so 
different possible flex structures and the accompanying rules are laid out here.   
For flex designs, the first priority to sort out is spacing between traces (dimension B in Figure 10-1), then pitch 
(total distance of dimensions A+B), then line width (dimension A). 
For traces that are under constant bias voltage, a space of 100um is recommended to prevent shorting. Also make 
sure to test the interaction of the solder paste and the encapsulation material under BHAST conditions (Biased 
Highly Accelerated Stress Test).  
Note: the larger the flex, the larger trace and space dimensions need to be.

Standard Impedance control is ±10%, while standard trace width control is +/-20%. 
 
Impedance is a function of surrounding dielectric thickness (PI plus adhesive), trace width, trace length, and trace 
pitch. Higher frequencies and quicker rise times will lead to more sensitive results. Always check with SI team to 
make sure engineering and Ops are on the same page. Frequently the only impedance value desired is the 
minimum value in the designated area. 
 
Figure 10-10 Impedance Measurement Graph 
 
The above Figure 10-10 Impedance Measurement Graph shows an impedance chart. On the left are values for a 
rise time of 32ns, on the right is 230ns. Notice that there is a drop outside of the specified area due to the contact 
pad which acts as a capacitor, dropping resistance (y axis), while on the far right the data turns vertical, indicating 
that the signal has hit the end of the trace (a travel time of 4.2x10-9 s ). If that occurs sooner one can use that value 
to find where an open is located on the trace. The value the SI team is concerned with is the minimum value.  
 
Section 11. Materials and Stackups 
Base Materials 
Flexible printed circuits used by Apple are typically polyimide based, however some high-speed applications 
require Liquid Crystal Polymers (LCP). Other flexible circuit materials, including PET or others, are not included in 
this design guide. Rigid-flexes are also not included in this design guide. Go to document 080-5589 for more 
information on rigid-flex design rules.

Base materials consist primarily of a polyimide film (which serves as a dielectric and flexible mechanical support) 
and copper foil. LCP and low loss PI follow this same structure as standard PI films. These are known as 
Adhesiveless PI because the PI and copper are bonded at a high temperature and therefore do not have adhesive 
between them.

Coverlay Materials 
Coverlay materials protect and insulate the external surfaces of an FPC.  The material consists of a polyimide sheet 
with an adhesive bonding sheet to facilitate lamination to the patterned copper surface of an FPC. Standard 
material is 0.012 mm PI and 0.015 mm or 0.025 mm adhesive.

Coverlay thickness nominal vs. final 
Coverlay is made at the nominal thickness but after the flex is fabricated and laminated all together into one 
stackup, the final thickness is different from the nominal. Coverlay will have a finished, post-lamination thickness 
thinner on designs where there the copper below the coverlay is mostly removed. The following formula is useful 
in calculating the adhesive thickness after lamination. This formula will not work when the copper thickness 
exceeds the adhesive thickness. This is designed as a “reference only” tool. 
Finished Adhesive Thickness=Starting Adhesive Thickness-[(1-Cu Density)*Cu Thickness]

Finished Adhesive = Adhesive thickness after lamination 
Starting Adhesive = Adhesive thickness before lamination 
Cu Density = Proportion of the layer to be filled that is covered with circuitry. Estimate 0.30 for a signal layer, 0.70 
for a ground layer or 0.50 if unknown 
Cu Thickness = Copper Thickness on the layer to be filled.

Finished thickness of the adhesive will depend on the finished thickness of the copper layer to which it is laminated 
as well as the copper pattern density.  For example, coverlay that is laminated to a solid copper plane will have a 
finished adhesive thickness very close to the starting thickness while coverlay laminated over a single thin trace 
may have a finished adhesive thickness of nearly zero, as most of it will move to the plane the copper nominally 
occupies.  In practice, the finished thickness will vary across the surface of an FPC. 
Adhesive thickness for use as bonding sheets 
Flexes constructed from more than one Flexible Copper Clad Laminate (FCCL) material require an adhesive to hold 
the layers of copper and PI together. The thickness of this bonding sheet adhesive varies depending on the copper 
used and the direction of the vias. See below for some recommendations on the minimum adhesive thicknesses. If 
the adhesive is too thin, you might see issues with voids, as shown below in the figure. The buttons around vias 
also locally increase the thickness of the copper, exacerbating the problem of adhesives too thin.

Photo-imageable Solder Mask Materials 
Photo-imageable solder mask materials (LPI) may be applied to areas where fine-pitch components are to be used 
or if better resolution of opening size or location is required. The base material will be coated with liquid photo-
imageable material and imaged to expose correct openings.  Finished thickness will vary in a manner similar to the 
adhesive used to bond coverlay, however, opening size and location will be nearly an order of magnitude more 
precise than ordinary coverlay. 
Photo-imageable solder masks are not intended for bending and should be kept clear from areas with anything 
more than the most minor flexing. It should also be noted that solder mask materials may shrink upon cure and 
could lead to warpage of the FPC if not properly supported by stiffener in the area of use. If a stiffener cannot be 
used to support the transition, another form of strain relief may be used such as glue dispensed at the transition 
location. When photo-imageable solder mask materials are used in combination with coverlay, the transition from 
solder mask to coverlay requires an overlap. It should also be noted that this area would be thicker than 
immediately adjacent areas due to the overlapping materials.  As a result, components/pad opening should be 
kept a minimum of 0.250mm from the coverlay edge, as shown below in Figure 10-6. See Coverlay and LPI section 
for more information.

The addition of photo-imageable solder mask material to a design adds cost as a result of the material itself and 
the processing required applying it.  The cost will generally not change as the application increases in area or in 
number of areas per side but will increase if both sides of a flex (top and bottom) use photo-imageable solder 
mask instead of just one side.  
Copper Foils and Copper Plating 
Two primary classes of copper foil are available.  The difference in method of manufacture of these foils results in 
different mechanical properties.   
Table 11-6 Copper foil characteristics and applications. 
The mechanical properties of HTE foils are practically uniform in every direction.  An older foil type, standard rolled 
annealed (RA) foils, are discouraged for use as the mechanical properties of (RA) foils will differ significantly by 
direction. Specifically, bending reliability in the rolled direction (MD) will be excellent but will be poor (typically 
worse than ED) across the rolled direction (TD). Standard Hyper Annealed (HA) foil will not demonstrate a 
directional bias. While HA is an improvement over RA, JX Nippon as introduced HA-V2 as a generally superior foil. 
HA-V2 has smaller crystal grains which allow for a rougher surface after soft (micro) etching. A rougher surface 
allows for better adhesion and thus less delamination. This comes with an increased price and a slight but tolerable 
decrease in flexibility. 
 
Any application where RA performance is desired or denoted, HA foil or HA-V2 should be used.  
Plating electrodeposited copper on HA foil will eliminate the bending reliability advantage HA foil has over most ED 
foils. HA copper with copper plating on top of the surface foil will have properties similar to ED foil.  Given the 
additional thickness, plating will reduce bending reliability when compared to un-plated foil. As a result, button 
plating (which plates only vias and a portion of the associated pad) is required for designs that need HA foil.  
Button plating results in a higher cost FPC and will not allow design rules as to be as dense as they would be 
otherwise.  See Error! Reference source not found. section for the applicable design rules.

Stiffeners 
Stiffeners are typically composed of a stiffening substrate and separate bonding adhesive.  Stiffener thicknesses 
typically range between 0.050mm and 0.500mm and the bonding adhesive between 0.025mm and 0.050mm. 
Stiffeners less than 0.100mm are generally made from polyimide while thicker stiffeners may be FR-4, stainless 
steel (SUS), or more exotic materials for specialty applications. Stainless steel stiffeners are strong, robust, and 
very clean which make them preferred over other stiffener materials if thickness allows.  
Stiffener placement is generally manual therefore stiffeners that can be punched at the same time as the flex 
outline are preferred so that edge alignment accuracy is maximized.  In most cases a PI (flex) stiffener can be 
punched with the flex circuit while FR4 and SUS stiffeners cannot be punched with the flex outline. Because of this, 
there will be a placement penalty in this case.  Stiffeners can be place directly on top of EMI shielding, however 
this is not preferred because the stiffener bond strength will decrease. Stiffeners that cannot be punched at the 
same time with board outline will need to be 0.200mm smaller than the actual flex circuit on all shared edges. 
When two separate stiffeners are in close proximity to each other the minimum space between them is 0.5 mm in

Surface Finish 
The preferred surface finish for flexes will vary by application.  See Surface Finishes section for more information.          
Shielding Techniques 
There are several different shielding options, which are selected based on, flexibility, impedance, thickness, cost, 
and EMI requirements. Copper planes are typically connected to ground with the use of vias while Silver/EMI 
shielding, and the Tatsuta films are attached to grounds by creating openings in the coverlay or adhesive material. 
The contact opening surface finish should be nickel and gold to prevent oxidation and better conductivity.

Openings must be created in the coverlay material so that the shielding material can electrically connect to the FPC 
circuitry as required by the circuit design. More than one opening per shield is recommended.

and the pressure at Silver/EMI shield lamination may force a short. Silver/EMI shield may encroach upon a coverlay 
to LPI transition area as long as it does not go over the LPI region and remains above the coverlay.

Low loss materials 
 
When designing flexes with low loss materials, the FCCLs and bonding sheets have different properties 
than standard PI. One of the biggest differences for bonding sheets is that they have lower adhesion 
than normal bonding sheets. To account for this, pre-baking to remove moisture before reflow is 
recommended to remove any excess moisture and prevent bubbling. Additionally, process control 
should be in place so the flex does not exceed 260°C. It is also recommended to add vent holes to the 
copper layers in any 3+ layer flex when using low loss bonding sheets. A sample design is shown below

Panelization 
Panelization is the term to describe the nesting of the flexes in the larger panel that they are cut from. The number 
of pieces per panel (pieces per square meter) and the amount of useful area (percent utilization) are two key 
metrics. With both, the higher the better. Panelization depends mostly on the flex outline, but also on the 
efficiency of the nesting. If the shape of the flex is rectangular or square, the panelization will likely be better than 
other shapes. Examples like star, cross, L, T etc. will generally lead to lower panelization. Lower panelization 
creates more raw material waste and a higher environmental impact. Each square meter of finished flex material 
uses roughly the same amount of energy to create, so the more flexes per square meter, the better. Poor 
panelization—among other hazardous waste pollution—increases handling, decreases output, and increases price. 
 
Avoid 
Prefer

Panelization for Complicated Flexes 
If possible, change from one complicated flex to two simpler flexes. A possibility is to use ACF bonding to connect 
the sub flexes, and see the ACF section for more details. Make sure to weigh the overall pros and cons for the 
single flex vs combination of two, but this can increase the panelization and thus panel usage rate. These do not 
always increase the panelization and might actually increase the overall cost, when including assembly and 
additional process steps. Other types of joints like ZIFs, B2Bs or hot bars are also possible. 
Another option is to add a fold in the flex to straighten “L” shaped flexes, as shown in Figure 12-3. The yellow panel 
is the same size for both.

Outline Features 
Outline features will ordinarily be punched using previously formed holes for alignment.  As a result, alignment to 
patterned features may be imprecise. When possible, stiffeners, adhesives, etc. should be selected and designed 
such that their outline coincides with the FPC outline in order to allow mass-placement and to avoid a stack-up of 
locational tolerances. 
Add an MCO Note to add Keep Out or Not Allowed Vestige Area for Micro-Joint Locations and see the Vestige Tab 
section of this document for more information. 
Board Outline Tools Selection  
There are many options for creating the final board outline. Tighter tolerance (outline to outline shape) will drive 
costs up. The tolerance of a board designated as a development product may be looser than that of a production 
board so selecting the correct board outline option will save money. In addition, the tighter tolerances will require 
a longer lead-time to get the outline tool created.

Section 15. PSAs, TSAs, and EMI shielding 
Adhesives, both pressure sensitive adhesives (PSAs) and temperature sensitive adhesives (TSAs) are used in many 
applications to attach the flex to other surfaces or materials. Common applications include for attaching stiffeners 
to flexes or for attaching flexes to the rest of the system. 
EMI shielding is most commonly a thin layer of silver with a thin adhesive for use in electrically shielding the 
sensitive flex wiring. See Shielding in the Materials section for more information

Silkscreen marking should be only used for traceability markings only. Silkscreen shall not be in bend region. See in 
figure shown with the silkscreen alignment discrepancy between part to part as example of why silkscreen should 
not be used as an alignment feature for any sensitive application. Silkscreen markings are about 30um in thickness.

Table 19-3 Surface Finish Categorical Rules 
Preferred Surface Finishes for different applications 
The preferred surface finish for flexes will vary by application.  See Apple document 080-2265 for more 
information. The preferences are tabulated below.  OSP may be used in combination with any other finish in order 
to protect solderable surfaces when mechanical contacts are also present.  If gold plated over nickel is used for 
solderable surfaces, the gold thickness should not exceed 0.075µm to prevent the solder from being brittle. 
 
ENIG is the most common surface finish and is best to be used in most applications.  
Application 
Preferred surface finishes (listed in order) 
Solderable Surfaces 
1. ENIG with flexible Nickel 
2. OSP 
Mechanical Contacts  
1. Electroplated Nickel/Gold (Hard) and ENIG with flexible Nickel 
ZIF Contacts or other 
insertion contacts 
1. ENIG  
2. Electroless Nickel/Gold (hard) 
ACF  
1. ENIG with Flexible Nickel  
2. Electroplated Direct Gold 
3. Electroless Nickel/Gold (soft) 
Hot Bar Pads  
1. ENIG with Flexible Nickel 
Wire bonding pads 
1. ENEPIG 
2. Electroless Nickel/Gold (soft) 
3. ENIG with flexible Nickel

Table 19-4 Surface Finish Preferences 
Plating bus bars/tie bars are required only when an electroplated surface finish is required on the contact fingers. 
The bus bars should be extended to the FPC edge and shall be no greater than half the width of the contact and no 
less than 0.075mm. When using the recommended ENIG with Flexible Nickel surface finish, no bus bar will be 
required. 
Both “hard” gold and “soft” gold are generally good protective finish for corrosive environments but require 
plating tie-bars and may not be suitable for all designs.  The “hard” variety used trace amounts of impurities such 
as cobalt to achieve increased hardness over pure gold, which is used for the “soft” variety.  In both hard and soft 
gold, nickel is plated before the gold. The underlying nickel is also available in more than one variety.  Flexible 
nickel underplate is required for all insertion connectors because the flexible nickel is less prone to cracking if the 
nickel is flexed during the insertion process.  Flexible nickel deposits may be electroless nickel or electroplated 
nickel but please consult your DFM support person before selecting electroplated soft nickel. 
Electroless Nickel Immersion Gold (ENIG) has a flat profile and does not require plating tie-bars but has higher risk 
of cracking if bent compared with Electroplated Nickel/Gold. All ENIG deposits must be formulated with flexible 
nickel and we also want a low gold/high nickel thickness for our deposit.  
When hand soldering, OSP with pre-soldered pads is preferred, though nickel/gold finish may also acceptable and 
should be used if pre-soldering is not available. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Section 20. ZIF Connectors 
Connectors 
We use often use Board to Board (B2B) connectors, Hot Bars, and Zero Insertion Force (ZIF) connectors. Please 
contact the ECAD librarian for the most up to date footprint designs. 
ZIF Connectors 
Dimensions for ZIF connectors including contact length and width, coverlay edge to FPC edge, FPC width, and the 
distance from FPC edge to the center of the outermost contacts should be designed as per the drawing associated 
with a particular ZIF connector or family of connectors. Please contact the ECAD librarian for the most up to date 
footprint designs.

1. Document Description
1.1.Scope 
This specification applies to flexible printed circuit boards (FPC) produced by chemically 
etching conductor patterns on insulating base material with interfacial connections 
established by mechanical or laser drilled holes in the insulating base material. The 
Flexible Printed Circuit Boards refer to single sided, double sided and multilayer with or 
without stiffener, EMI shielding and PSA. 
1.2.Purpose 
The purpose of this specification is to establish the requirements for single sided, 
double sided and multilayer flexible printed circuit boards procured as a substrate or as 
an assembly for Apple Inc. products.  This document also establishes some required 
practices for quality control, reporting and record keeping by the fabrication suppliers of 
FPC to be used in Apple, Inc. products.

1.7.Responsibilities 
Apple, Inc. will be responsible for the content of the ODB++ data and associated 
deliverables made available for the fabrication of FPCB’s for Apple products.  If Gerber 
data is provided by Apple, it is for reference only and may not be used for fabrication of 
the FPCB.  Only ODB++ design data is to be used for FPCB fabrication. 
The FPCB fabrication supplier is responsible to verify that the data, including all 
documentation obtained from Apple for the fabrication of FPCB’s is the same revision 
level as that specified by the FPCB purchase order.  The FPCB fabrication supplier will 
also be responsible to verify the instructions/data received from Apple are proper for the 
fabrication of the FPCB as requested by Apple, Inc.
The FPCB fabrication supplier will be responsible for the fabrication of FPCB’s in 
compliance with this specification unless an authorized Apple representative has 
provided deviations from these requirements in writing.
It is the fabrication supplier’s responsibility to target design nominal for all features:
• The actual average value of laser-drilled-PTH size (but not BVH and IVH size), solder 
mask opening size, metal pad size, and trace width shall meet ±5 µm or ±10% of 
target design nominal value whichever is less. Design tolerances are intended only 
to accommodate for process variations. It is not acceptable to change one feature 
to enhance another. For example, it is not acceptable to target a lower copper 
thickness to make it easier to etch fine traces.
• Controlled Impedance design nominal is the target impedance. The trace width may 
be modified to meet the impedance requirement, and must meet specification. Refer 
to section 5.3. Impedance Control.
• Nominal of an unbalanced tolerance is defined as the target value. For example, 2.0 
is the target value in the specification 2.0 +0.05 -0.08 mm.
1.8.Notification 
Potential deviations or modifications from Apple specification or design data, as noted in 
this document, must be approved in writing by an authorized Apple representative prior 
to the initiation of FPCB fabrication by the supplier.
The FPCB fabrication supplier shall contact the designated Apple representative at 
dfflex@group.apple.com and pcb-verification@group.apple.com with regard to any 
recommendations for improvements to design, layer structure, etc.  Apple requires a 
complete DRC/DFM report and stack-up diagram including dielectric constant and all 
material thicknesses, including copper, be provided for Apple review at 
dfflex@group.apple.com and pcb-verification@group.apple.com.
The report should follow and contain a minimum of :
•
Use Apple Engineering Deviation Request form 080-5473, Revision 02

1.9.Modifications  
Modifications to the ODB++ design data provided by Apple must be approved in writing 
by an authorized Apple representative unless the modification will continue to satisfy all 
of the specification limits for the ODB++ data or unless they are among the following list 
of acceptable modifications to Apple ODB++ data:
•
Scaling – Incorporate scaling factors to compensate for material shrinkage/
expansion in the FPCB fabrication process
•
Artwork – Modification for smooth traces and trace taper / tear drop. FPCB 
fabrication supplier must perform design rule check to verify no violation. See 
detail artwork modification specification in section 5 .
The FPCB fabrication supplier shall submit plots of the final manufacturing design data 
with approved edits to Apple. 
Any material change must highlighted specifically and got approved from Apple 
authorized person or they are non-approved deviation.
1.10.Regulatory Requirements 
All FPC shall be in compliance with UL 796 and the UL file (summarising FPC stack up 
and UL logo for each project) shall be in accordance with that document as well as the 
FPCB design database. The UL file shall include the following elements:
• UL/cUL recognition mark for US and Canada
• Manufacturer’s UL file number
• FPC material designation as certified by UL, both:
• 105°C MOT 
• Flammability rating, minimum UL 94 V-0 or VTM-0 unless otherwise specified in 
the README file or ODB++ data.
The FPCB fabrication supplier is responsible for the maintenance of UL certification and 
must show proof of UL compliance as well as material traceability, to Apple DRI team, 
including EPM and Safety team DRI, for all sustaining and new projects APN.
For all new projects APN:
• Application: as soon as FPCB fabrication supplier receives MCO or ODB++ for the 
first time (regardless of the build name, POC, P0, P1, P2, C6, C5, etc.), FPCB 
Fabrication supplier shall immediately apply for UL certification, and communicate 
timeline to Apple DFM team.
• Certification: FPCB fabrication supplier shall get the UL approval / UL certification / 
UL file number before EVT. If not feasible, FPCB fabrication supplier shall highlight to 
Apple DFM team, and provide schedule until completion. 
The FPCB fabrication process shall comply with P/N 069-0135, Apple Specification, and 
Regulated Substances.

2. Material
2.1.Material Details 
The stack-up detail showing layer-to-layer construction for FPCB’s will be specified in 
the drill drawing layer of the ODB++ data.
Additional detail, which specifies localized thickness requirements and/or the presence 
or absence of specific dielectric layers will be present in the MCO.
2.2.Dielectric Materials 
All dielectric material shall conform to UL designation with a flammability rating of UL 
94V-0 or UL 94 VTM-0 as applicable.  All dielectric material shall also conform to 
IPC-4202, IPC-4203, IPC-4204 as applicable.
For dielectric materials (except adhesives) shall have a minimum glass transition 
temperature minimum (Tg) of 140°C as measured in accordance with IPC-TM-650 
2.4.24.1 and a minimum decomposition temperature (Td) of 320°C as measured in 
accordance with IPC-TM-650 2.4.24.6. Adhesives shall be selected as appropriate for 
the FPC based on application and must at a minimum withstand the reliability 
requirements as outlined in this document.
Additional dielectric base materials, necessary to meet electrical, thermal and/or 
mechanical requirements, must either be specified in the ODB++ data or approved in 
writing by an authorized Apple representative prior to inclusion in the multilayer stack up 
of an Apple FPCB.
The insulation resistance of the Flexible Copper Clad Laminates (FCCL), bonding 
sheet, coverlay and Solder Mask shall be greater than 100 MΩ in accordance with 
IPC-6013 - 3.9.4 requirement and test condition refer to IPC-TM 650 - 2.6.3
The peel strength of the copper clad laminate, as received by the FPCB fabrication 
Supplier, shall be greater than or equal to six pounds/in (1.05 N/mm) when tested in 
accordance with IPC-TM-650-2.4.9. Any deviation to the peel strength specified requires 
approval from Apple authorized person. This peel strength requirement does not apply 
to materials specified in ODB++, MCO, and ERS, which have been selected 
intentionally.
A proper copper adhesion treatment and multilayer lamination process shall be 
employed such that the resulting inner layer bond strength is greater than or equal to 
four pounds/in when tested in accordance with IPC-TM-650-2.4.40.

2.3.Multilayer Stack-up 
• The individual copper and dielectric thickness requirements specified on the drill 
drawing layer of the ODB++ data are intended to reflect requirements for finished 
FPC.
• Multilayer stack-ups must be designed to 1) Minimize bending stresses, 2) Optimize 
dimensional stability, and 3) Optimize FPCB costs and material availability.
• The FPCB fabrication supplier shall provide a complete stack-up accordingly with 
different layer zone including dielectric constant and all finished material thicknesses, 
including copper foil, solder mask, coverlay, adhesive, EMI shielding, Stiffener, PSA, 
Surface finishing type and plated copper.  Along with material vendor name and part 
number the type of copper foil and type of adhesive shall be disclosed, as well as 
whether a button-via plating process, selective plating process or a panel plated 
copper process is used. 
2.4.Dielectric Constant and Loss Tangent 
The dielectric constant of the dielectric material shall be 2.9 to 3.6 when measured at 1 
GHz and the loss tangent shall be maximum 0.040 when measured at the same. When 
black coverlay is required the dielectric constant of the dielectric material shall be less 
than 5.2 when measured at 1 GHz and the loss tangent shall be maximum 0.100 when 
measured at the same. Any deviation to the specification require approval from Apple 
authorized person.
2.5.Surface Imperfections
FPCB surface shall be inspected in accordance with 080-03267 Apple FPC Cosmetic 
Spec and/or dedicated project cosmetic spec provided by Apple authorized person.
For any items non specified in that document, FPCB fabrication supplier shall refer to 
IPC-6013 and shall confirm class 2 requirements, or specifications based on application 
from Apple documents and Apple authorized DFM/SQE.
2.6.Copper Foil 
HTE copper foil shall be used for Apple FPC unless otherwise specified on the stack-up 
detail of the drill drawing layer in the ODB++ data or the MCO.
2.7.Halogen-free Materials 
“Halogen-free” refers to FPC laminate; solder mask, coverlay, legend ink, and surface 
finish, which shall comply with Apple P/N 069-1857, Apple Halogen-Free Specification.
Halogen-free materials shall comply with all other Apple material specifications.
2.8.Environmental Specifications 
All Apple FPCB’s must comply with the following Apple specifications:
• P/N 069-1111, Apple RoHS Compliance Specification
• P/N 069-0135, Apple Regulated Substances Specification
Finished Apple FPCB’s shall not contain Triphenyl phosphate (TPP) or red phosphorus.

3.4.HDI Stack-up Requirements 
FPC that require the use of high density interconnect (HDI) technology must meet the 
requirements of the multilayer stack-up detail in the drill drawing layer of the ODB++.
HDI FPCB fabricated for Apple must meet the capture and target pad dimension and 
location requirements, blind and buried via dimension and location requirements, as 
well as through hole via dimension and location requirements as specified in the ODB++ 
data.
The FPC fabrication supplier will not use acrylic adhesive that will be exposed in the 
hole wall of blind via without approval in writing by an authorized Apple representative 
prior to FPCB fabrication by the supplier.

4. Copper Plating 
4.1.Copper Plating Quality and Integrity 
Regardless of the plating type, all plated holes, exposed pad areas, as well as any other 
areas designated for plating must demonstrate complete coverage with no evidence of 
separation, cracks, voids or exposed underlying metal or material. 
Nodules or rough plating in plated through hole shall not reduce the hole diameter 
below the minimum limit defined in the ODB++ file or MCO .
4.2.Plated Copper purity 
Minimum purity for plated copper on Apple FPCs shall be 99.5% as determined by IPC-
TM-650 2.3.15.

referring to Finished Goods level thickness, application to both button and panel 
copper plating.

process control to guaranty these thicknesses (e.g. sample size, etc.).
• Supplier shall select and define the plating type unless specified in MCO or ODB ++

5.6.Trace Tapers/Tear Drops 
Trace tapers may not exceed the diameter of via pad and must only extend a distance 2 
time equal to the via pad diameter from the center of via pad to the conductor interface 
before the taper must meet the width dimensions of the affected conductor. See Figure 
5.6.1.
The addition of trace tapers may not violate any Apple specified requirements and the 
fabrication supplier must perform a design rule check after trace tapers are added to 
confirm no violation.
Tear drop shall be added to the pad/trace interface for all traces and pads including 
square and rectangular pads, the “D” dimension shown below may equal the longest 
edge of the pad or It shall capture by cover layer or photo imageable cover layer by 
minimum of 50 um. See Figure 5.6.2

6. Photo-Imageable Soldermask and Coverlay
6.1.Materials and Application 
Soldermask utilised for Apple FPC may be liquid photo imageable and Apple personal 
approved photo imageable dry film (must conform to specific thickness requirements) 
must be applied over bare copper only. 
All polymeric soldermask, coverlays must be in compliance IPC 6012 or IPC 6013 as 
applicable when tested according to IPC-TM-650 2.6.3.1 Class H, Moisture and 
Insulation Resistance 100Mohm.
6.2.Photo Imageable Soldermask Thickness 
Soldermask thickness shall be an absolute minimum of 4 µm at a pattern edge and an 
absolute maximum of 39 µm over all conductive surfaces is required by the soldermask 
layer of the ODB++ data.
The height difference between an exposed metallized area and the upper surface of the 
soldermask immediately adjacent to the metallized area may not exceed 25 µm.
6.3.Photo Imageable Soldermask Registration  
The finished size of an exposed photo imageable solder mask defined metal pad shall 
not deviate more than 25 µm from the size specified in the Apple ODB++ data.
The maximum soldermask registration tolerance for any FPCB is ±50 µm from the same 
layer copper pattern. 
All metallized areas, including for example the etched foot of a feature, which are shown 
to be covered with solder mask shall not be exposed and shall be covered by solder 
mask  as specified in 6.4

7. Surface finishing / Coating 
7.1.Material thickness and Application type  
Overall surface finishing or selective surface finishing requirement shall be specific in 
surface finishing layer of ODB++ or MCO. Reference thickness shall be see in this 
section .
7.2.Electroless Nickel/Immersion Gold  
When specified on the fabrication drawing, all exposed areas of the entire board shall 
be plated with gold over nickel over base copper. ENIG with flexible Nickel is require on 
all Apple FPCBs where ENIG is specified as surface finish. 
Immersion plated gold shall have an average thickness between 0.025 µm (1µ”) thick 
and 0. 075 µm (3µ”) thick with an absolute minimum of 0.025 µm (1µ”) thick and an 
absolute maximum of 0.100 µm (4µ”) thick and be 99% pure.
Electroless nickel shall be a minimum thickness of 2 µm and a maximum thickness 6µm 
with exception to iff fingers contacts that must meet an absolute minimum thickness of 
2µm and a maximum thickness 3.75 µm.
7.3.Electrolytic Gold / Nickel Plating
All FPCB edge connection contacts shall be plated with gold over nickel over base 
copper unless otherwise stated by the ODB++ or MCO. 
Plated gold used for edge connection contacts shall conform to AMS 2422 or ASTM 
B488.
Plated gold shall be a minimum of 0.25 µm thick and 99% pure. The hardness of the 
plated gold shall be 130 to 200 Knoop.
Plated nickel shall conform to the requirements of AMS 2403.
Plated nickel used for edge connection contacts shall be a low stress deposit, with an 
absolute minimum thickness of 2 µm and a maximum thickness 6µm with exception to 
Ziff fingers contacts that must meet an absolute minimum thickness of 2µm and a 
maximum thickness 3.75 µm.
7.4.Organic Solderability Preservative (OSP) 
When identified as the surface finish for an Apple FPCB, OSP coating over bare copper 
shall have a minimum thickness of 0.1 µm verified by a repeatable technique.

7.5.Electroless Nickel/Electroless Palladium/Immersion Gold (ENEPIG) 
When specified on the fabrication drawing, all exposed areas of the entire board shall 
be plated with gold over palladium over nickel over base copper. Gold plating shall not 
be applied over bare copper.  
Immersion plated gold shall be 0.09 µm – 0.20 µm thick and 99% pure. Electroless 
palladium shall be a minimum of 0.03µm thick and a maximum of 0.10µm. Electroless 
nickel shall be a minimum of 4.0 µm thick and a maximum of 8.0µm
7.6.Direct Electrolytic Gold 
When identified as the surface finish for an Apple FPCBs, electrolytic Gold over bare 
copper shall be minimum of 0.4 µm and maximum of 1µm and be 99% pure.

8. Hot Bar Pad Design 
8.1.Material selection 
FPC with hot bar application, Dielectric base material selection shall be stand hot bar 
bonding temperatures 320°C for 10 sec. Clarity / Transparency of PI material must meet 
section 8.6 specification. Any deviation requires approval from Apple authorized person.
8.2.Dimension specification  
Specification limits to be applied to all pads within the hot bar footprint area. 
Fabrication suppliers must be able to demonstrate Cpk > 1.33 for items at each hot bar 
footprint area present in the MCO level design. The mean value may be based on the 
95% confidence limits from a sample for sample sizes less than 32pcs.  
Term and Definition use in this section :
ASRO : Section A Solder Resist Opening  
BSRO : Section B Solder Resist Opening

8.3.Hot Bar Pad Solder Mask Thickness 
Specification limits to be applied to all pads within the hot bar footprint area. 
8.3.1.Solder Mask Thickness for Panel Plated or Unplated Pads
• NSMD pads
• The mean value of solder mask height (A) above the exposed metal surface 
must be less than 0.002 mm
• The absolute maximum height (A) of solder mask above and below the 
exposed metal surface may not exceed 0.010 mm.
• This shall not apply to the specific locations with a trace between pads.
• SMD pads
• The solder mask height (B) may not exceed 0.020 mm above the exposed metal 
surface. 
• Solder mask thickness (B*) on the covered portion of the pad, including pad’s 
edge, must be more than 0.004 mm.

8.4.Pre-soldered Thickness (After Reflow) 
￼
The Measurement Zone is defined as the middle 50% of the exposed pad width. 
The Tolerance Zone is defined as the area 0.030mm to 0.060mm above the exposed pad. 
Within the Measurement Zone, the height of solder after reflow must be completely within the 
Tolerance Zone.
Cross-section shall be the official method of measurement and vendors must be able to show 
Cpk > 1.33. 
Vendors may propose a non-destructive method of solder height verification. An acceptable 
method must demonstrate correlation to cross-section measurements with R2 > 0.90 for values 
within the Tolerance Zone.

9. Mechanical and Dimensional Requirements
9.1.FPCB Thickness 
The finished total thickness of the FPCB shall be measured after the completion of all 
fabrication operations required for the specific design to match the ODB++ data and 
shall be measured from the solder mask/coverlay surface over conductors of one side 
of the FPCB to solder mask/coverlay over conductors on the other side. Section with 
different layer stack up required separate measurement for each section. See Figure 
9.1 
    
￼
An FPC with gold plated edge connectors (sometimes referred to as gold fingers or gold 
tabs) may have a separate thickness requirement measured over the gold plated 
connector area and should be verified in addition to the solder mask/coverlay to solder 
mask/coverlay thickness requirement for the rest of the FPCB.
FPCB thickness tolerance shall be a maximum of ±10% of the nominal FPCB thickness 
unless otherwise specified on the drill-drawing layer of the ODB++ data or the MCO.

10.Stiffener Application 
10.1.Material  
Material selection and thickness requirement shall be specific in stiffener layer of ODB+
+ data or MCO. Stiffener profile shall be either punch out together with outline or shall 
be apply individually. 
SUS used for conductivity, the conductivity resistance value shall be less than 5 Ohm or 
as specified in MCO. SUS with conductive adhesive shall be Nickel coated with 
thickness range of 1µm ~ 3 µm. 
The combined bow and twist of each finished FPCB at stiffener portions shall not 
exceed 0.75% for applications that use SMT components and 1.5% for all other 
applications along the linear dimension of the FPC.

11.Legend Marking
11.1.Application and Information  
Vendor markings shall be present on all Apple FPCB’s. Marking location shall be accordance 
with ODB++ data and or MCO description. Vendor markings must be located so as to be 
visible after assembly.
At no time shall any legend marking be applied over, or make contact with, any exposed 
metallized surface or at bending area zone shown to be free of legend on the silkscreen 
or ink jet layer of the ODB++ data unless otherwise specified. 
If the FPCB design is so small that there is not enough room to apply all fabrication 
supplier markings, the order of precedence (of markings to apply) as space permits 
shall be:
1st
= Fabrication supplier Logo (or equivalent mark that can identified 
fabrication supplier) and APN
2nd
= Lot number 
3rd
=  Sheet #
4th
= Cavity number
      5th    =  Date code  
 
6th
= UL mark/logo
Any deviation require approval from Apple authorized person.
Part numbers other than the Apple part number may not be applied to Apple FPC, 
unless specifically authorized in writing by an authorized person.
Country of manufacture (adjacent to the manufacturers logo) shall be marked by legend 
on the FPC only if specified in the ODB++ data.
The date code shall at a minimum identify the Week and Year of manufacture, and shall 
be applied either by legend or laser by a permanent non-conductive ink.
The UL mark applied to the FPC shall correspond to the applicable qualification file the 
fabrication supplier has for the FPC and shall be marked by legend.
Any other markings as required by Apple ODB++ data and/or pertinent specifications, 
shall be printed, or affixed to the FPCB as required.

12.Cleanliness
12.1.Cleanliness 
Cleanliness shall be in accordance with the requirements of Apple PCB Fab Process 
Cleanliness Requirements 080-02184, and applicable section of IPC 6013. 
12.2.In-Process Cleanliness 
All phases of the FPCB fabrication must be kept free from sources of contamination or 
degradation. 
Prior to the application of soldermask and/or Coverlay, the uncoated FPCB shall meet 
the requirements of IPC-A-600 paragraph 19, when measured per IPC-TM-650.
12.3.Ionic Contamination 
Ionic contamination for all Apple FPCB’s must be less than 4.5 micrograms NaCl per 
square inch. 
refer to Apple Bare FPC General Continuous Quality Reliability Assurance and OQC 
Metrology Requirement 080-02999
12.4.Wire bonding pad Cosmetic and Cleaning 
To ensure Bondability and reliability of wire bonding FPCBs shall be clean , the cleaning 
specification shall agree upon fabrication supplier and contract manufacturer where the 
wire bonding process perform . 
The wire bonding pad include deformation , foreign material , dent , bump , scratches 
and or solder paste are not acceptable and cause of rejection. Refer to 080-03267 
Apple FPC incoming and outgoing Cosmetic Criteria for criteria details.

13.Electrical Requirements 
13.1.Electrical Test  
Test shall be completed in accordance with all applicable IPC -TM-650 specification 
requirements unless otherwise specific by MCO or Apple authorized person. 
Two-sided simultaneous (“clamshell”) test or a flying probe which can probe both sides 
is required for all Apple FPCB’s, which have net terminations on both sides of the 
FPCB. 
Test programs shall be extracted from the Apple reference net list or ODB++ data. 
As specified by an authorized Apple representative, 100% test coverage will be required 
for FPCB’s used for engineering development. Report documenting the Test coverage 
and production lot test result shall be furnished to Apple’s designated representative 
upon request. 
If flying probe is used, all adjacent nets including those in adjacent layers must be 
tested for short circuit. Where the resistance requirement of under 1 ohm or trace width 
and spacing less than 50 μm it shall use 4 wire testing method . 
13.2.Impedance Test  
Unless otherwise specified on the drill drawing, test for impedance shall be performed in 
accordance with IPC-TM-650 2.5.5.7. 
The FPCB shall be fabricated such that the impedance requirements as specified on the 
multilayer stack-up detail from the drill-drawing layer of the ODB++ data are properly 
satisfied. A test configuration, which allows for a conductive path to each applicable 
signal layer, which shall have trace width equivalent to the affected layer, must be 
provided in order to verify the impedance values. The length of the conductive path may 
be modified to allow for FPCB dimensions if the length and test equipment capability 
satisfy IPC-TM-650 2.5.5.7.

subcontracted to a third party supplier.

in such inspections and/or tests as considered necessary to ensure conformance with 
applicable requirements. 
All drilled holes shall be inspected to ensure complete extension between the layers 
specified subsequent to the de-smear process.

to verify that each delivered FPCB conforms to the requirements as stipulated in this

16.4.Shelf Life 
A. FIFO system in accordance to the manufacturing date code must be 
applied on product shipment sequence. The maximum shelf life for Apple 
Bare FPC is 12 months started from FPC surface plating finishing to 
Assembly soldering (excluding Tin plating, Tin plating surface finishing shelf 
life to be specified by independent project QCP).
B. The Bare FPC finish good shall be follow the packaging and storage 
requirement accordance to 080-01409
C. The 12 month shelf life is referred to Bare FPC prior to component 
assembly and soldering. FPC with less than 4 months shelf life to assembly 
soldering may not be shipped to an Apple contract manufacturer without the 
test stated according to Bare FPC shelf life Extension 080-02864
D. Bare FPC older than 12 months to assembly may be accepted subject to 
test requirements according to Bare FPC shelf life Extension 080-02864
E. Overdue/Ageing Bare FPC  must be subjected to the test requirements with 
guarantee letter approved by Apple SQE before shipment to contract 
manufacturer. This requirements are only applicable if the initial packaging 
shows no sign of tempering. If desiccant and moisture indicator card has 
shown sign of moisture, Bare FPC shall be scrapped. Bare FPC that pass 
ageing test shall be extended for another 6 months with a maximum limit of 
2 years from the Surface plating finishing. 
F. Guarantee letter and bundled reports of each lot must be shared with 
contract manufacturer SQE and Apple provided distribution list (if any) 
before shipment. Shipment boxes shall be clearly labeled with *ageing test 
done*, the date tested and the number of re-test done to date. 
G. Any Bare FPC in storage manufacturers which has a shelf life > 2 year and 
expire / obsolete need to follow “obsolete” . “Expire” deposition matrix 
according to 080-4112 requirement.
16.5.Retained samples 
FPCB fabrication supplier should have retaining finish good Bare FPC sample for each 
manufacturing lot at fabrication factory or site. 
• For MP build
• Each lot minimum 5 pieces. The finish good bare FPC sample should not be 
subject to any CQRA test item and be functionally good. Sample shall be kept 
until part EOL or 5 years whichever longer. 
• for NPI build
• All lots built leftover, all defective samples which did not ship to downstream and 
all the quality test samples [from CQRA, IPQC, FAI, etc] shall kept until part EOL 
or 5 years whichever longer.
Retained sample shall be packed and stored in the conditions as 16.2 and 16.3

17.Shipping Requirements 
17.1.Shipping Requirements  
FPCB’s must be shipped to Apple’s designated electronic contract manufacturer within 
one week of the date of manufacture unless otherwise specified in writing by Apple an 
authorized representative. 
All package must contain desiccant packets to ensure the absence of moisture, 
moisture colour indicator must be placed in all packages. When shipping bare FPCB’s 
to Apple or a Contract Manufacturer the FPCB’s shall be oriented in the same direction 
within the package. A stiffener must be placed in each package to protect the FPCB’s 
from any unintended bending / creasing caused from handling or the crushing force of 
the vacuum sealing process. 
The preferred weight for each package is less than or equal to 25 pounds. All packages 
within boxes must be labeled with the following 
Traceability Markings
Date codes and lot codes 
Manufacturer 
Apple Part Number and Revision 
Quantity of both FPCB’s and Panel Arrays, if applicable. 
No. of X-Outs per panel, if applicable 
Warranty conditions shall be as specified on the Apple purchase order. 
The FPCB fabrication supplier shall be liable for all FPCB shipments until the shipment 
is properly received and accepted by Apple, Apple’s designated subcontract 
manufacturer, or other subcontractor location designated by Apple.

1.
Purpose
The purpose of this specification is to establish acceptability requirements for any new and existing equipments 
(including tooling/jig/fixture), new manufacturing methods (process parameter, materials and chemical, process flow) and 
new material not used in any Apple’s rigid, rigid-flex and flex printed circuit boards before. Those equipments, methods 
and materials shall be qualified by the procedures defined in these guidelines.
2.
Scope
This specification applies to the quality requirements of rigid, rigid-flex and flex printed circuit boards.  
3.
Responsibilities
The rigid, rigid-flex and flex PCB fabrication supplier will be responsible for the fabrication of PCB’s and the qualification of new and 
existing equipments in compliance with this specification unless an authorized Apple representative has provided deviations from 
these requirements in writing.
4.
References
5.
Definition
POR equipment: Plan of Record machine that finished qualification
POR material: Plan of Record material that finished qualification
PCB: Printed Circuit Board (including rigid, rigid-flex, flex and substrate)
